content.text:
The Chinese Remainder Theorem (CRT) allows us to solve systems of 
simultaneous congruences.

content.title: Chinese Remainder Theorem
content.content:
Let $n_1, n_2, \ldots, n_k$ be pairwise coprime positive integers, and 
let $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_k$ be any integers. Then the system of congruences
$$
x &\equiv a_1 \pmod{n_1} \\
x &\equiv a_2 \pmod{n_2} \\
 &\;\;\vdots \\
x &\equiv a_k \pmod{n_k}
$$
has a unique solution modulo $N = n_1 n_2 \cdots n_k$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For each $i$, let $N_i = N/n_i$. Since $\gcd(N_i, n_i) = 1$, there exists 
$M_i$ with $N_i M_i \equiv 1 \pmod{n_i}$.

Set $x = \sum_{i=1}^k a_i N_i M_i$.

For each $j$: if $i \neq j$, then $n_j \mid N_i$, so $N_i M_i \equiv 0 \pmod{n_j}$. 
And $N_j M_j \equiv 1 \pmod{n_j}$. Therefore
\[
x \equiv a_j N_j M_j \equiv a_j \pmod{n_j}.
\]

*Uniqueness:* If $x$ and $x'$ are both solutions, then $n_i \mid (x - x')$ 
for all $i$. Since the $n_i$ are pairwise coprime, $N \mid (x - x')$.

content.title: Solving with CRT
content.solution:
$N = 3 \cdot 5 \cdot 7 = 105$.

$N_1 = 35$, $N_2 = 21$, $N_3 = 15$.

Find inverses: $35 \cdot 2 = 70 \equiv 1 \pmod{3}$, so $M_1 = 2$.\\
$21 \cdot 1 = 21 \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$, so $M_2 = 1$.\\
$15 \cdot 1 = 15 \equiv 1 \pmod{7}$, so $M_3 = 1$.

$x = 2 \cdot 35 \cdot 2 + 3 \cdot 21 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 15 \cdot 1 
= 140 + 63 + 30 = 233 \equiv 23 \pmod{105}$.

Check: $23 = 7 \cdot 3 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{3}$ $\checkmark$\\
$23 = 4 \cdot 5 + 3 \equiv 3 \pmod{5}$ $\checkmark$\\
$23 = 3 \cdot 7 + 2 \equiv 2 \pmod{7}$ $\checkmark$

content.text: The CRT has many applications in number theory and computer science.

content.title: Computing with CRT
content.problem:
To compute $17^{100} \pmod{35}$:

Since $35 = 5 \times 7$, compute separately:

$17 \equiv 2 \pmod{5}$, so $17^{100} \equiv 2^{100} \equiv (2^4)^{25} \equiv 1^{25} \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$.

$17 \equiv 3 \pmod{7}$, so $17^{100} \equiv 3^{100} \equiv (3^6)^{16} \cdot 3^4 \equiv 1 \cdot 81 \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$.

Now solve $x \equiv 1 \pmod{5}$, $x \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$.

Testing: $x = 1, 6, 11, 16, 21, 26, \ldots$ The first with $x \equiv 4 \pmod{7}$ is $x = 11$.

Therefore $17^{100} \equiv 11 \pmod{35}$.
